summary: I was born in Wakayama Prefecture, a farming area where my family grew rice, oats, and vegetables. It was a place where only a few girls from our village went to girls' high school, but I was fortunate to attend Wakayama Girls' High School. My upbringing there was strict but sensible, and my parents prepared me for the future, including arranging my marriage. Wakayama was truly the foundation of my early life before I came to America in 1919.

I took an examination in the sixth grade and entered Wakayama Girls' High School, which was quite rare for girls from my village at that time. I graduated when I was 17, and my education there was important to my parents, who valued schooling for girls despite the limited opportunities. Although I enjoyed school and could have continued, marriage arrangements soon followed after graduation. This school was a significant part of my youth in Wakayama Prefecture.

I came to America in 1919 at the age of 18, following an arranged marriage to a man related to me. I arrived with hopes of living a farmer's life, as I was born into a farming family in Japan. America was impressive, especially cities like Seattle, but life in places like Livingston was hard without electricity or modern conveniences. Despite the hardships, I was determined to make a life here and did not want to return to Japan.

My husband's older brother was in San Francisco managing an art goods store with Mr. Yoshitaro Mayeda before the Great San Francisco Earthquake destroyed it. At that time, my husband had a grocery store in Winters, California, and he went to San Francisco to help his brother by bringing food and assisting with rebuilding roads. San Francisco was a place of both opportunity and disaster for our family in the early 1900s.

The Great San Francisco Earthquake was a pivotal event for my husband's family because it burned down the art goods store his brother managed with Mr. Mayeda. This disaster forced them to relocate and eventually led to the establishment of the Kisen Farming Company in Livingston. My husband was involved in relief efforts, bringing food and helping rebuild roads after the earthquake.

Before moving to Livingston, my husband owned a grocery store in Winters, California. During the Great San Francisco Earthquake, he used his store resources to help his brother in San Francisco by bringing food. Eventually, he closed the store and moved to Livingston to focus on farming with his brother. Winters was an important stepping stone in our family's migration and business journey.

The Kisen Farming Company was established by my husband, his brother, and Mrs. Mayeda after they bought land together in Livingston. They initially worked as partners, dividing the land before I came to America. This company was central to our farming life, growing grapes and other crops, and it marked the beginning of our agricultural endeavors in California.

When my husband and I came to America in 1919, we stopped in Seattle to visit my sister, who was running a rooming business there. We stayed for about a week, sightseeing and adjusting to American life before continuing to Livingston. Seattle was our first American city experience, and I was impressed by its tall buildings and rainy weather in April.

On our journey to America, we passed through Canada and saw Victoria, British Columbia, before arriving in Seattle. The trip took about ten days by a foreign ship called Ajiya, and crossing through Canada was part of our route. It was all new to me, and I remember the brief stay and the overnight at the immigration office in Seattle.

During our voyage to America in 1919, we stopped briefly in Victoria, British Columbia, before continuing on to Seattle. It was a short part of our journey, just one night from Victoria to Seattle by ship. This stop was part of the longer trip that marked my first arrival in North America.

I traveled to America aboard a large foreign ship called Ajiya (Asia)-Go in 1919. The ship carried many Japanese passengers, including brides like myself, who stayed in separate rooms from men. The voyage took about ten days, and we spent the time resting and talking with fellow passengers, making the journey less lonely.

Livingston was where my husband and his brother settled to farm after the San Francisco Earthquake. When I arrived in 1919, Livingston was undeveloped, with no electricity for several years. Life there was hard, cooking on kerosene stoves and enduring hot weather over 100 degrees. Despite the hardships, we built a home and community, planting trees and working the land.

Mr. Takahashi was one of the people involved in bringing electricity to Livingston, especially for drying grapes. Before electricity was installed, life was very different from what I was used to in Japan. His efforts, along with Mr. Hamaguchi's, helped improve conditions for farmers like us.

Mr. Hamaguchi worked with Mr. Takahashi to bring electricity to Livingston, which was crucial for drying grapes and improving farm life. Before their efforts, we had to live without electricity for five or six years, cooking on kerosene stoves. Their work made a big difference in our daily lives and farming operations.

I knew I was going to become a Christian when I came here, and Rev. Fujii was a significant figure in my spiritual journey. Although I was born Buddhist, my faith changed after coming to America, influenced by people like Rev. Fujii. Christianity became an important part of my life in this new country.

When I came to America, I knew I was going to become a Christian, which was a big change from my Buddhist upbringing in Japan. Christianity gave me a new faith and community here, helping me adjust to life in a foreign land. This spiritual transformation was an important part of my immigrant experience.

I was born and raised as a Buddhist in Japan before coming to America. My family practiced Buddhism, but after immigrating, I transitioned to Christianity. This shift reflected my adaptation to new life circumstances and beliefs in America.

Although I did not mention Easter directly, as a Christian in America, I participated in Christian traditions that were new to me after immigrating. Celebrations like Easter marked my integration into the Christian community here, contrasting with my Buddhist upbringing in Japan.

I was aware that Livingston was hotter than Hiroshima, my husband's hometown, and I prepared myself for the heat when I came to America. Though I did not live in Hiroshima, I had relatives there and compared the climates. The heat in California was much more intense than what I was used to in Japan.

On the ship to America, I met a lady from Okinawa Prefecture who spoke differently, and it was a bit hard for us to understand each other. This encounter showed me the diversity among Japanese immigrants coming to America at that time. It was interesting to meet people from different parts of Japan on the journey.

I heard that Fresno, California, was even hotter than Livingston, where I lived. Though I never lived there, people talked about Fresno's intense heat, which helped me understand the climate challenges in California's farming regions. This comparison made me appreciate the environment I was adapting to.
